From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American writer and filmmaker (born 1954)

"Straczynski" redirects here. For the asteroid, see [8379 Straczynski](/wiki/8379_Straczynski "8379 Straczynski").

**Joseph Michael Straczynski** ([/strəˈzɪnski/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-JMS_1994_0623-1) born July 17, 1954) is an American filmmaker and [comic book writer](/wiki/Comic_book_writer "Comic book writer"). He is the founder of Synthetic Worlds Ltd. and Studio JMS and is known as the creator of the science fiction television series *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")* (1993–1998) and its spinoff *[Crusade](/wiki/Crusade_(TV_series) "Crusade (TV series)")* (1999), as well as the series *[Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah_(TV_series) "Jeremiah (TV series)")* (2002–2004) and *[Sense8](/wiki/Sense8 "Sense8")* (2015–2018).[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is the [executor](/wiki/Executor "Executor") of the [estate](/wiki/Literary_estate "Literary estate") of [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Straczynski wrote the psychological drama film *[Changeling](/wiki/Changeling_(film) "Changeling (film)")* (2008) and was co-writer on the martial arts thriller *[Ninja Assassin](/wiki/Ninja_Assassin "Ninja Assassin")* (2009), was one of the key writers for (and had a cameo in) Marvel's *[Thor](/wiki/Thor_(film) "Thor (film)")* (2011), as well as the horror film *[Underworld: Awakening](/wiki/Underworld:_Awakening "Underworld: Awakening")* (2012), and the apocalyptic horror film *[World War Z](/wiki/World_War_Z_(film) "World War Z (film)")* (2013). From 2001 to 2007, Straczynski wrote [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics")' *[The Amazing Spider-Man](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man "The Amazing Spider-Man")*, followed by runs on *[Thor](/wiki/Thor_(Marvel_Comics) "Thor (Marvel Comics)")* and *[Fantastic Four](/wiki/Fantastic_Four_(comic_book) "Fantastic Four (comic book)")*. He is the author of the *[Superman: Earth One](/wiki/Superman:_Earth_One "Superman: Earth One")* trilogy of graphic novels, and he has written *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(comic_book) "Superman (comic book)")*, *[Wonder Woman](/wiki/Wonder_Woman "Wonder Woman")*, and *[Before Watchmen](/wiki/Before_Watchmen "Before Watchmen")* for [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics"). Straczynski is the creator and writer of several original comic book series such as *[Rising Stars](/wiki/Rising_Stars_(comics) "Rising Stars (comics)")*, *[Midnight Nation](/wiki/Midnight_Nation "Midnight Nation")*, *[Dream Police](/wiki/Dream_Police_(comics) "Dream Police (comics)")*, and *Ten Grand* through [Joe's Comics](/wiki/Joe%27s_Comics "Joe's Comics").

A prolific writer across a variety of media and former journalist, Straczynski is the author of the autobiography *Becoming Superman* (2019) for HarperVoyager, the novel *Together We Will Go* (2021) for Simon & Schuster, the instructional *Becoming a Writer, Staying a Writer* (2021) for Benbella Books, and the novel *The Glass Box* (2024) for Blackstone Publishing. In 2020 he was named Head of the Creative Council for the comics publishing company Artists, Writers and Artisans.

Straczynski is a long-time participant in [Usenet](/wiki/Usenet "Usenet") and other early computer networks, interacting with fans through various online forums (including [GEnie](/wiki/GEnie "GEnie"), [CompuServe](/wiki/CompuServe "CompuServe"), and [America Online](/wiki/America_Online "America Online")) since 1984. He is credited as being the first TV producer to directly engage with fans on the Internet[[4]](#cite_note-JMS_2000_0925-4)[[5]](#cite_note-JMS_2007_0522-5) and to allow viewer viewpoints to influence the look and feel of his show (see [*Babylon 5*'s use of the Internet](/wiki/Babylon_5%27s_use_of_the_Internet "Babylon 5's use of the Internet")). Two prominent areas where he had a presence were GEnie and the newsgroup [rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated](/wiki/Rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated "Rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Early and personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early and personal life")]

Straczynski was born in [Paterson, New Jersey](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey"), and is the son of Charles Straczynski, a manual laborer, and Evelyn Straczynski (née Pate).[[7]](#cite_note-7) His father was an alcoholic and often violent to his family. His mother suffered from depression.[[8]](#cite_note-8) He was raised in [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"); [Kankakee, Illinois](/wiki/Kankakee,_Illinois "Kankakee, Illinois"); [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), Texas; [Chula Vista, California](/wiki/Chula_Vista,_California "Chula Vista, California"), where he graduated from high school;[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), California.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Straczynski's family religion was [Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") although he is an atheist. He has [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") ancestry[[12]](#cite_note-ref1-12) and his grandparents lived in the area which today belongs to [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") and fled to the United States from the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_of_1917 "Russian Revolution of 1917"); his father was born in the United States and has lived in Poland, Germany and Russia.[[12]](#cite_note-ref1-12)

Straczynski began his postsecondary education at [Southwestern College](/wiki/Southwestern_College_(California) "Southwestern College (California)"), where he was mentored by Bill Virchis and received an [Associate of Arts](/wiki/Associate_of_Arts "Associate of Arts") degree. (Additionally, the two-year institution later awarded him an honorary degree.) Thereafter, he transferred to nearby [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University") (SDSU), where he received a [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") degree with a double major in [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") and [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") (and minors in [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and literature). While at SDSU, he wrote for the student newspaper, *The Daily Aztec*.

Straczynski met [Kathryn M. Drennan](/wiki/Kathryn_M._Drennan "Kathryn M. Drennan") while they were at SDSU. They moved to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in 1981, married in 1983,[[13]](#cite_note-13) separated in 1999, and they were divorced in 2001.[[14]](#cite_note-IGN-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Straczynski has [Asperger syndrome](/wiki/Asperger_syndrome "Asperger syndrome").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Straczynski had a voluntary [vasectomy](/wiki/Vasectomy "Vasectomy") when he turned 21, and wrote about the experience in the January 28, 1983 edition of the *[Los Angeles Reader](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Reader "Los Angeles Reader")*.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1970s–1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s–1980s")]

#### Early work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early work")]

Straczynski began writing plays, having several produced at [Southwestern College](/wiki/Southwestern_College_(California) "Southwestern College (California)") and [San Diego State University](/wiki/San_Diego_State_University "San Diego State University") before publishing his adaptation of *Snow White* with Performance Publishing.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Several other plays were produced around San Diego, including *The Apprenticeship* for the Marquis Public Theater. During the late 1970s, Straczynski became the on-air entertainment reviewer for KSDO-FM and wrote several radio plays before being hired as a scriptwriter for the radio drama *[Alien Worlds](/wiki/Alien_Worlds_(radio) "Alien Worlds (radio)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-AlienWorlds-20)[[14]](#cite_note-IGN-14) He produced his first television project in San Diego, *Marty Sprinkle* for [KPBS-TV](/wiki/KPBS_(TV) "KPBS (TV)") as well as worked on the [XETV-TV](/wiki/XETV-TDT "XETV-TDT") project *[Disasterpiece Theatre](/wiki/Disasterpiece_Theatre "Disasterpiece Theatre")*.[[21]](#cite_note-Gale-21) He worked as a journalist for the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* as a special San Diego correspondent and worked for *[San Diego Magazine](/wiki/San_Diego_Magazine "San Diego Magazine")* and *The San Diego Reader*, and wrote for the *[Los Angeles Herald-Examiner](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Herald-Examiner "Los Angeles Herald-Examiner")*, the *Los Angeles Reader, TV-Cable Week,* and *[*People*](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* magazine.[[14]](#cite_note-IGN-14) Straczynski wrote *The Complete Book of Scriptwriting* for *[Writer's Digest](/wiki/Writer%27s_Digest "Writer's Digest")*. Published in 1982, the book is often used as a text in introductory screenwriting courses,[[22]](#cite_note-HUP037_Scriptwriting-22)[[23]](#cite_note-CW3029_Writing_for_Radio-23) and is in its third edition.

He spent five years from 1987 to 1992 co-hosting the *[Hour 25](/wiki/Hour_25 "Hour 25")* radio [talk show](/wiki/Talk_show "Talk show") on KPFK-FM Los Angeles with [Larry DiTillio](/wiki/Larry_DiTillio "Larry DiTillio"). During his tenure, he interviewed [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter"), [Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman"), [Ray Bradbury](/wiki/Ray_Bradbury "Ray Bradbury"), [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison") and other writers, producers, actors and directors. In 2000, Straczynski returned to radio drama with *[The City of Dreams](/w/index.php?title=The_City_of_Dreams&action=edit&redlink=1 "The City of Dreams (page does not exist)")* for [scifi.com](/wiki/Syfy#Websites_and_divisions "Syfy").

#### Work in animation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Work in animation")]

Straczynski was a fan of the cartoon *[He-Man and the Masters of the Universe](/wiki/He-Man_and_the_Masters_of_the_Universe "He-Man and the Masters of the Universe")*. He wrote a [spec script](/wiki/Spec_script "Spec script") in 1984 and sent it directly to [Filmation](/wiki/Filmation "Filmation").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Filmation purchased his script and several others, and hired him on staff. During this time he became friends with [Larry DiTillio](/wiki/Larry_DiTillio "Larry DiTillio"), and when Filmation produced the *He-Man* spinoff *[She-Ra: Princess of Power](/wiki/She-Ra:_Princess_of_Power "She-Ra: Princess of Power")*, they worked as story editors on the show.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Straczynski and DiTillio worked to create an animated version of *[Elfquest](/wiki/Elfquest "Elfquest")*, but that project fell through when CBS attempted to retool the show to appeal to younger audiences.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

While working on *Jayce*, Straczynski was hired to come aboard the [Len Janson](/wiki/Len_Janson "Len Janson") and [Chuck Menville](/wiki/Chuck_Menville "Chuck Menville") project to adapt the movie *[Ghostbusters](/wiki/Ghostbusters "Ghostbusters")* to an animated version called *[The Real Ghostbusters](/wiki/The_Real_Ghostbusters "The Real Ghostbusters")*. When Janson and Menville learned that there was not only a 13-episode order but a 65-episode syndication order as well, they decided that the workload was too much and that they would only work on their own scripts.[[28]](#cite_note-GBDVD-28) DIC head [Jean Chalopin](/wiki/Jean_Chalopin "Jean Chalopin") asked Straczynski to take on the task of story editing the entire 78-episode block as well as writing his own scripts.[[28]](#cite_note-GBDVD-28) After the show's successful first season, consultants were brought in to make suggestions for the show, including changing Janine to a more maternal character, giving every character a particular "job" (Peter is the funny one, Egon is the smart one, and Winston, the only black character, was to be the driver), and to add kids into the show.[[28]](#cite_note-GBDVD-28) Straczynski left at this point, Janson and Menville resuming the story editing job for the second network season. Straczynski then began development on a show called *[Spiral Zone](/wiki/Spiral_Zone "Spiral Zone")* but left after only one script, taking his name off the series, because management drastically altered his conception of the show.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

#### Live action and network shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Live action and network shows")]

After leaving animation, Straczynski freelanced for *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1985_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1985 TV series)")* writing an episode entitled "What Are Friends For" and, for [Shelley Duvall](/wiki/Shelley_Duvall "Shelley Duvall")'s *[Nightmare Classics](/wiki/Nightmare_Classics "Nightmare Classics")*, adapting *[The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde](/wiki/The_Strange_Case_of_Dr._Jekyll_and_Mr._Hyde "The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde")*, which was nominated for a [Writer's Guild](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") Award.

Straczynski was offered the position of story editor on the syndicated live-action science fiction series *[Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future](/wiki/Captain_Power_and_the_Soldiers_of_the_Future "Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future")*. Straczynski constructed a season long arc with lasting character changes and wrote a third of the scripts himself. After one season, the toy company [Mattel](/wiki/Mattel "Mattel") demanded more input into the show, causing Straczynski to quit. He recommended DiTillio to take over the job as story editor for a second season, but the toy company financing fell through and that season was never produced.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Soon after, the [1988 Writers Guild of America strike](/wiki/1988_Writers_Guild_of_America_strike "1988 Writers Guild of America strike") began. Straczynski met [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison") during this time and later became friends with him.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) Straczynski is an executor of Ellison's collected works.

After the strike ended, the producers of the new *Twilight Zone* needed to create more episodes to be able to sell the series into syndication with a complete 65-episode package. They hired Straczynski as executive story editor to fill in the remaining number of needed episodes.

After leaving *Twilight Zone*, his agent of the time asked him to pitch for the show *[Jake and the Fatman](/wiki/Jake_and_the_Fatman "Jake and the Fatman")*.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Initially wary, Straczynski finally did and was hired on as an executive story consultant under [Jeri Taylor](/wiki/Jeri_Taylor "Jeri Taylor") and [David Moessinger](/w/index.php?title=David_Moessinger&action=edit&redlink=1 "David Moessinger (page does not exist)"). When Taylor and Moessinger left the show, Straczynski left too as an act of solidarity.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

When Moessinger was hired as executive producer for *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*, he offered Straczynski a job as co-producer. Straczynski joined *Murder, She Wrote* for two seasons and wrote seven produced episodes. Moessinger and Straczynski moved the protagonist, Jessica Fletcher, from the sleepy Maine town of [Cabot Cove](/wiki/Cabot_Cove "Cabot Cove") to New York City to revitalize the show. The move effectively brought the show back into the top ten from the mid-thirties where it had fallen.

Straczynski wrote one episode of *[Walker, Texas Ranger](/wiki/Walker,_Texas_Ranger "Walker, Texas Ranger")* for Moessinger between the pilot episode for *Babylon 5* and the start of its first season.[[35]](#cite_note-JMS_1996_0305-35)

Straczynski wrote an adaptation of [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson")'s *The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde* for the [Showtime](/wiki/Showtime_(TV_network) "Showtime (TV network)") network, which was nominated for a Writers Guild of America award,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and a *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")* movie, *[Murder, She Wrote: A Story to Die For](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote:_A_Story_to_Die_For "Murder, She Wrote: A Story to Die For")*, which he produced.

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1990s")]

#### *Babylon 5* and *Crusade*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Babylon 5 and Crusade")]

In late 1991, [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") contracted with Straczynski and [Doug Netter](/wiki/Douglas_Netter "Douglas Netter") as partners to produce *Babylon 5* as the flagship program for the new [Prime Time Entertainment Network](/wiki/Prime_Time_Entertainment_Network "Prime Time Entertainment Network").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Straczynski and Netter hired many of the people from *Captain Power*, as well as hiring Ellison as a consultant and DiTillio as a story editor. *Babylon 5* won two [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award"), back-to-back [Hugo Awards](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award"), and dozens of other awards. Straczynski wrote 92 of the 110 [episodes](/wiki/List_of_Babylon_5_episodes "List of Babylon 5 episodes"), as well as the pilot and five television movies. The show is a character-driven [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera") and features an intentional emphasis on realism in its portrayal of space operations. It pioneered extensive use of [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") for its [special effects](/wiki/Special_effect "Special effect"). *Babylon 5* was produced and broadcast for five seasons completing Stracynski's planned story arc.

He wrote the outlines for nine of the [canonical](/wiki/Canon_(fiction) "Canon (fiction)") *Babylon 5* novels, supervised the three produced *Babylon 5* telefilm novelizations (*In the Beginning*, *Thirdspace*, and *A Call to Arms*), and is the author of four *Babylon 5* short stories published in magazines, not yet reprinted (as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit)).

In 2005, Straczynski began publishing his *Babylon 5* scripts.[[38]](#cite_note-38) This process ended in June 2008, with the scripts no longer being available from the end of July of that year. His scripts for the television movies were published for a limited time in January 2009.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### Joe's Comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Joe's Comics")]

Straczynski has long been a comic fan and began writing comics in the late 1980s. His work in comics includes the adaptations of *[Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future](/wiki/Captain_Power_and_the_Soldiers_of_the_Future "Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future")*, *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone "The Twilight Zone")*, *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(DC_Comics) "Star Trek (DC Comics)")*, and *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5_Novels,_novelizations,_short_stories,_and_comic_books "Babylon 5 Novels, novelizations, short stories, and comic books")*. In 1999 he created *[Rising Stars](/wiki/Rising_Stars_(comics) "Rising Stars (comics)")* for [Top Cow](/wiki/Top_Cow "Top Cow")/[Image Comics](/wiki/Image_Comics "Image Comics").

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2000s")]

#### Marvel Comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Marvel Comics")]

[Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") signed him to an exclusive contract, beginning with a run on *[The Amazing Spider-Man](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man "The Amazing Spider-Man")*, from 2001 to 2007. He took over the series with Volume 2 issue #30 ([cover dated](/wiki/Cover_date "Cover date") June 2001).[[40]](#cite_note-40) Straczynski and artist [John Romita Jr.](/wiki/John_Romita_Jr. "John Romita Jr.") crafted an acclaimed story for *The Amazing Spider-Man* #36 (Dec. 2001) in response to the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[41]](#cite_note-41) He wrote or co-wrote several major Spider-Man story arcs including "[Spider-Man: The Other](/wiki/Spider-Man:_The_Other "Spider-Man: The Other")",[[42]](#cite_note-42) "[Back in Black](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Back_in_Black "Spider-Man: Back in Black")",[[43]](#cite_note-43) and the infamous "[One More Day](/wiki/One_More_Day_(comics) "One More Day (comics)")".[[44]](#cite_note-44) He later wrote several other Marvel titles including *[Supreme Power](/wiki/Squadron_Supreme_(Supreme_Power) "Squadron Supreme (Supreme Power)")*,[[45]](#cite_note-45) *[Strange](/wiki/Strange_(comic_book) "Strange (comic book)")*,[[46]](#cite_note-46) *[Fantastic Four](/wiki/Fantastic_Four_(comic_book) "Fantastic Four (comic book)")*, *[Thor](/wiki/Thor_(Marvel_Comics) "Thor (Marvel Comics)")*,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and mini-series featuring the Silver Surfer and a "What If" scenario, *[Bullet Points](/wiki/Bullet_Points_(comics) "Bullet Points (comics)")*.

#### *Jeremiah*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Jeremiah")]

Straczynski ran *[Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah_(TV_series) "Jeremiah (TV series)")*, loosely based on the Belgian [post-apocalyptic](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction") comic *[Jeremiah](/wiki/Jeremiah_(comics) "Jeremiah (comics)")*, from 2002–2004. Straczynski ran the series for two seasons but was frustrated with the conflicting directions that [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM") and [Showtime](/wiki/Showtime_(TV_network) "Showtime (TV network)") wanted from the show,[[48]](#cite_note-48) and even used the pseudonym "Fettes Grey" for the first time since *Spiral Zone* on one of the scripts. In the second season, Straczynski decided to leave the show if things did not improve,[[49]](#cite_note-49) and the show ended after two seasons.

#### *Changeling*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Changeling")]

Straczynski wrote *[Changeling](/wiki/Changeling_(film) "Changeling (film)")*, a psychological drama film based partly on the ["Wineville Chicken Coop" kidnapping and murder case](/wiki/Wineville_Chicken_Coop_Murders "Wineville Chicken Coop Murders") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California"). Directed by [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood"), produced by [Ron Howard](/wiki/Ron_Howard "Ron Howard"), and starring [Angelina Jolie](/wiki/Angelina_Jolie "Angelina Jolie"), the film premiered in 2008 and subsequently received eight nominations for the [BAFTA Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), including a nomination for Best Original Screenplay.[[50]](#cite_note-bafta_2009-50) The first draft script was written in eleven days, after Straczynski figured out "how to tell" the story,[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51) which ended up being the shooting draft, after Eastwood declined to make any changes.[[52]](#cite_note-52) It was optioned immediately by Howard, who at first intended to direct the film but later stepped down after scheduling conflicts.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51)

At first, Straczynski expressed disbelief at the story,[[53]](#cite_note-53) but spent a year researching the case, compiling over six thousands pages of documentation. Straczynski claimed that 95% of the script's content came from the historical record,[[54]](#cite_note-54) and went through the script with [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures")'s legal department, providing attribution for every scene so the film would be described as "a true story" rather than "based on" one. On how his journalistic background helped him write the film, Straczynski stated:

> It was hugely important. Usually, when you're asked to tell a true story in film, there's already an article or something where the legwork's been done. In this case, there was nothing available. It was all primary research—City Hall archives, county courthouse archives, criminal records, hospital records. I just sifted through stuff, often spending a whole day paging through records just to find one reference.[[55]](#cite_note-Time;_Changeling-55)

#### Feature screenwriter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Feature screenwriter")]

Straczynski announced on February 23, 2007, that he had been hired to write the feature film adaptation of [Max Brooks](/wiki/Max_Brooks "Max Brooks")'s novel *[World War Z](/wiki/World_War_Z "World War Z")* for [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") and production company [Plan B Entertainment](/wiki/Plan_B_Entertainment "Plan B Entertainment"), taking screen story credit on the finished film.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 2008, Straczynski wrote a draft of *[Ninja Assassin](/wiki/Ninja_Assassin "Ninja Assassin")* for [Joel Silver](/wiki/Joel_Silver "Joel Silver"), which he completed in just 53 hours.[[57]](#cite_note-Kaufman-57) The film was produced by [The Wachowskis](/wiki/The_Wachowskis "The Wachowskis") and released on November 25, 2009.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Straczynski is credited as "story writer" along with [Mark Protosevich](/wiki/Mark_Protosevich "Mark Protosevich") for the 2011 film, *[Thor](/wiki/Thor_(film) "Thor (film)")*.[[60]](#cite_note-60) He makes a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in the film,[[61]](#cite_note-thorcameo-61) his first appearance in a movie and his second appearance as an actor (the first being "[Sleeping in Light](/wiki/Sleeping_in_Light "Sleeping in Light")", the final episode of *Babylon 5*).[[62]](#cite_note-62) Straczynski was part of the writers room (along with [Terry Rossio](/wiki/Terry_Rossio "Terry Rossio"), Patrick McKay, J. D. Payne, Lindsey Beer, Cat Vasko, [T. S. Nowlin](/wiki/T._S._Nowlin "T. S. Nowlin"), and Jack Paglen) to develop *[Godzilla vs. Kong](/wiki/Godzilla_vs._Kong "Godzilla vs. Kong")*, though was uncredited on the final script.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

#### DC Comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: DC Comics")]

When his exclusive contract with Marvel ended, he was announced as the writer for a run on *[The Brave and the Bold](/wiki/The_Brave_and_the_Bold "The Brave and the Bold")* for [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics").[[64]](#cite_note-64) He collaborated with artist [Shane Davis](/wiki/Shane_Davis "Shane Davis") on an out-of-continuity original graphic novel starring [Superman](/wiki/Superman "Superman") titled *[Superman: Earth One](/wiki/Superman:_Earth_One "Superman: Earth One")*.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) The story features a young Superman and focus on his decision about the role he wants to assume in life.[[67]](#cite_note-67) On March 8, 2010, it was announced he would be taking over writing duties for the monthly *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(comic_book) "Superman (comic book)")* title[[68]](#cite_note-68) with a story arc entitled "[Grounded](/wiki/Superman:_Grounded "Superman: Grounded")", and the *[Wonder Woman](/wiki/Wonder_Woman "Wonder Woman")* title, beginning with issues 701 and 601 respectively.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) Less than a year later he was asked by DC to step away from both titles in order to concentrate on the second volume of *Superman: Earth One* and handed them over to [Chris Roberson](/wiki/Chris_Roberson_(author) "Chris Roberson (author)") and [Phil Hester](/wiki/Phil_Hester_(comics) "Phil Hester (comics)") to finish his Superman and Wonder Woman stories respectively. In 2012, Straczynski wrote *[Before Watchmen](/wiki/Before_Watchmen "Before Watchmen"): Dr. Manhattan* drawn by [Adam Hughes](/wiki/Adam_Hughes "Adam Hughes") and *Before Watchmen: Nite Owl* drawn by [Andy Kubert](/wiki/Andy_Kubert "Andy Kubert") and [Joe Kubert](/wiki/Joe_Kubert "Joe Kubert").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) A second volume of *Superman: Earth One* was released later that same year.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

At [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") in 2015, DC Comics announced *The Flash: Earth One*, a new graphic novel of its [Earth One](/wiki/Earth_One_(DC_graphic_novel_series) "Earth One (DC graphic novel series)") line to be written by Straczynski, set to be published in 2016.[[74]](#cite_note-DCFlash-74)[[75]](#cite_note-YehlFlash-75)[[76]](#cite_note-ArrantFlash-76) As of 2023, it has still not been published.

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2010s")]

Straczynski's Julius Schwartz Lecture at [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), November 2015

### Studio JMS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Studio JMS")]

In July 2012, J. Michael Straczynski announced the launch of [Studio JMS](/wiki/Studio_JMS "Studio JMS") to produce TV series, movies, comics and, in the future, games and web series.[[77]](#cite_note-Deadline-77) On March 27, 2013, Netflix announced they would produce the show *[Sense8](/wiki/Sense8 "Sense8")* with Studio JMS and [The Wachowskis](/wiki/The_Wachowskis "The Wachowskis"), which aired on June 5, 2015, and earned a season 2 announcement by August 10, 2015.[[78]](#cite_note-JMS_2013_03_27-78)

#### Joe's Comics revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Joe's Comics revival")]

The [Joe's Comics](/wiki/Joe%27s_Comics "Joe's Comics") line was revived at [Image Comics](/wiki/Image_Comics "Image Comics") in 2013 with the launch of *Ten Grand* drawn by [Ben Templesmith](/wiki/Ben_Templesmith "Ben Templesmith")[[79]](#cite_note-79) and *Sidekick* drawn by [Tom Mandrake](/wiki/Tom_Mandrake "Tom Mandrake").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Dynamite Entertainment announced in July 2013 a new 12 issue *The Twilight Zone* comic book series penned by Straczynski.[[81]](#cite_note-81) The series ran for its projected 12 issues, from December 2013 to February 2015, with art by Guiu Vilanova. Straczynski was announced as the writer of *[Terminator Salvation: The Final Battle](/wiki/Terminator_Salvation:_The_Final_Battle "Terminator Salvation: The Final Battle")*, a 12 issue comic book series from Dark Horse Comics, along with artist Pete Woods.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

#### *Sense8*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Sense8")]

*[Sense8](/wiki/Sense8 "Sense8")*, a science fiction television series created by Straczynski and [The Wachowskis](/wiki/The_Wachowskis "The Wachowskis") was ordered straight-to-series by Netflix in March 2013.[[83]](#cite_note-83) *Sense8'*s first season debuted in June 2015 on Netflix, from [Studio JMS](/wiki/Studio_JMS "Studio JMS") and [Georgeville Television](/w/index.php?title=Georgeville_Television&action=edit&redlink=1 "Georgeville Television (page does not exist)"). Straczynski executive produced and co-wrote all 12 episodes of the first season with fellow creators, executive producers, and directors Lilly and Lana Wachowski.[[78]](#cite_note-JMS_2013_03_27-78) In August 2015, Netflix renewed *Sense8* for a second season.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2020s")]

#### Artists Writers and Artisans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Artists Writers and Artisans")]

In 2020, Straczynski was named head of the Creative Council for the new comics publishing company [AWA](/wiki/AWA_Studios "AWA Studios"), where he was responsible for creating the shared universe used by many of its writers.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Following the publication of his autobiography *Becoming Superman* in 2019 from [HarperVoyager](/wiki/HarperVoyager "HarperVoyager"), the novel *Together We Will Go* was published in 2021 by Simon & Schuster's Scout Press, and the instructional *Becoming A Writer, Staying A Writer* was published in 2021 by Benbella Books.[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 2024 he published the novel The Glass Box (2024) Blackstone Publishing.

#### Return to *Babylon 5*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Return to Babylon 5")]

In 2022, Straczynski announced that a reboot of his series *Babylon 5* was in the works at The CW, with the pilot script written by him. Following a buy-out of the CW by NextStar and strike action by the WGA, work on the project was stalled.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In 2023, it was announced by Straczynski that a fully animated *Babylon 5* feature film, *[Babylon 5: The Road Home](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Road_Home "Babylon 5: The Road Home")*, had been produced in secret and was scheduled for release in the summer of that year, reunited the surviving members of the original cast. It was released in August 2023.

#### Return to Marvel Comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Return to Marvel Comics")]

Across 2021 and 2022, Straczynski contributed short stories to several anthology and milestone editions of comics published by Marvel Comics, this led to him writing a mystery six issue event mini-series for the publisher. In June 2023, it was announced Straczynski would write the next volume of *[Captain America](/wiki/Captain_America "Captain America")*, beginning in September of that year.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Straczynski also returned to the *Spider-Man* franchise around this time, contributing a short story to the anthology mini-series *Spider-Man: Black Suit & Blood* as well as penning guest appearances by the character in storylines for *Captain America*.

In October of 2024, Straczynski confirmed on social media that he would be departing *Captain America* in December of that year, while the mystery six-part event announced some years back was ultimately revealed that same month as being an anthology series pairing up two different 'unlikely' Marvel characters per issue.

Strazynski would announce in spring of 2025 that he would be returning to Spider-Man to pen a five-issue mini-series, *[The Amazing Spider-Man: Torn](/w/index.php?title=The_Amazing_Spider-Man:_Torn&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Amazing Spider-Man: Torn (page does not exist)")*, set during Peter Parker's days spent at Empire State University with Gwen Stacy, Mary Jane Watson, and Harry Osborn. The book would launch in October.

## Unrealized projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=J._Michael_Straczynski&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Unrealized projects")]

In 2004, Straczynski was approached by [Paramount Studios](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") to become a producer of the *[Star Trek: Enterprise](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Enterprise "Star Trek: Enterprise")* series. He declined, believing that he would not be allowed to take the show in the direction he felt it should go.[[89]](#cite_note-JMS_2004_0623-89) He did write a treatment for a new *Star Trek* series with colleague [Bryce Zabel](/wiki/Bryce_Zabel "Bryce Zabel").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

After both *Babylon 5* and *Jeremiah* ended, Straczynski transitioned to working as a feature film screenwriter. In 2006, he was hired to write a feature film based on the story of [King David](/wiki/King_David "King David") for Universal by producers [Erwin Stoff](/wiki/Erwin_Stoff "Erwin Stoff") and [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman").[[91]](#cite_note-91) In June 2007, it was announced that Straczynski had written a feature screenplay for the *[Silver Surfer](/wiki/Silver_Surfer "Silver Surfer")* movie for Fox, the production of which would depend on the success of the *[Fantastic Four: Rise of the Silver Surfer](/wiki/Fantastic_Four:_Rise_of_the_Silver_Surfer "Fantastic Four: Rise of the Silver Surfer")*.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Additionally, he has written a script for [Tom Hanks](/wiki/Tom_Hanks "Tom Hanks")' Playtone Productions and Universal Pictures called *[They Marched into Sunlight](/wiki/They_Marched_into_Sunlight "They Marched into Sunlight")* based upon the Pulitzer nominated novel of the same name and an outline by [Paul Greengrass](/wiki/Paul_Greengrass "Paul Greengrass"), for Greengrass to direct, should it get a greenlight.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In June 2008, *[Daily Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* named Straczynski one of the top Ten Screenwriters to Watch. They announced Straczynski was writing *[Lensman](/wiki/Lensman "Lensman")* for [Ron Howard](/wiki/Ron_Howard "Ron Howard") (to whom he had sold a screenplay entitled *[The Flickering Light](/w/index.php?title=The_Flickering_Light&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Flickering Light (page does not exist)")*), that he was selling another spec, *Proving Ground*, to [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise") and [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists").[[57]](#cite_note-Kaufman-57) In October 2008, it was announced that Straczynski was engaged to pen a remake of the science fiction classic *[Forbidden Planet](/wiki/Forbidden_Planet "Forbidden Planet")*.[[94]](#cite_note-HR-2008-10-31-94) In the fall of 2009, it was reported that Straczynski was writing a movie titled *[Shattered Union](/wiki/Shattered_Union "Shattered Union")* for [Jerry Bruckheimer](/wiki/Jerry_Bruckheimer "Jerry Bruckheimer") and Disney. The screenplay, based on the video game of that name, concerns itself with a present-day American [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war").[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In October 2012, Valiant Entertainment announced a live-action feature film adaptation on its comic book series *Shadowman*, written and executive produced by Straczynski.[[97]](#cite_note-97) *The Flickering Light*, Straczynski's directorial debut, was announced in February 2013, with the World War II drama set to be written and produced by Straczynski through his Studio JMS.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Straczynski and Studio JMS optioned [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison")'s short story ["Repent, Harlequin!" Said the Ticktockman](/wiki/%22Repent,_Harlequin!%22_Said_the_Ticktockman "\"Repent, Harlequin!\" Said the Ticktockman"), who granted the option only after reading a finished screenplay written by Straczynski.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

At San Diego Comic-Con in 2014, it was announced that Straczynski and Graphic India would team up with [Chernin Entertainment](/wiki/Chernin_Entertainment "Chernin Entertainment") to produce a feature film adaptation of his upcoming graphic novel *Titans*, to be written and produced by Straczynski, through Studio JMS.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Straczynski was hired to adapt *[Red Mars](/wiki/Red_Mars "Red Mars")* for [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_(TV_network) "Spike (TV network)"), based on the [Kim Stanley Robinson](/wiki/Kim_Stanley_Robinson "Kim Stanley Robinson") novels, with [Vince Gerardis](/w/index.php?title=Vince_Gerardis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vince Gerardis (page does not exist)") as producer.[[101]](#cite_note-101) In December 2015, Spike TV gave a 10-episode straight-to-series order to *Red Mars*, set to premiere in January 2017, with Straczynski serving as writer, executive producer, and showrunner through [Studio JMS](/wiki/Studio_JMS "Studio JMS"), and production set to begin in Summer 2016.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) On March 25, 2016 *Deadline* reported that Straczynski had left his position as showrunner with [Peter Noah](/wiki/Peter_Noah "Peter Noah") replacing him but he too left due to creative differences with Spike. Spike has put the series on hold for further development.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

On July 30, 2021, Straczynski expressed an interest in becoming showrunner of the [BBC Television](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television") series *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")*, following the departure of [Chris Chibnall](/wiki/Chris_Chibnall "Chris Chibnall"), confirming his representatives had been contacting the BBC.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) On August 19, Straczynski confirmed contact had been made with the BBC, but that the organization had already begun their own selection process and were not seeking new candidates.[[107]](#cite_note-107) On September 24, 2021, the BBC confirmed former showrunner [Russell T Davies](/wiki/Russell_T_Davies "Russell T Davies") would return,[[108]](#cite_note-108) which Straczynski commended.[[109]](#cite_note-109)
